''Ardea'', 69, 205-208.</ref> In south-western Africa, they range up to as far north as the southern parts of the [[Republic of the Congo]] and the [[Democratic Republic of the Congo]], through most of [[Namibia]] (excluding the coastal regions) and northern [[South Africa]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name= Penhallurick/> In east Africa, their distribution is more or less continuous from southern [[Sudan]], [[Eritrea]] and inland [[Somalia]] down to [[South Africa]] as far as the region of the city of [[Durban]].<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref>Layard, E. L. (1884). ''The birds of South Africa''. Bernard Quaritch.</ref><ref>Lynesc, A. H. (1925). XV.— ''On the Birds of North arid Central Darfur, with Notes on the West‐Central Kordofan and North Nuba Provinces of British Sudan (Part IV.)''. ''Ibis'', 67(2), 344-416.</ref><ref>Wilhelmi, F. K., & Kaariye, H. Y. ''Bird observation in the Ogaden Region Somali Regional State,/Ethiopia A Contribution to the Identification of Important Bird Areas''.</ref>

This species inhabits mainly [[savanna]] with scattered trees and [[Thorns, spines, and prickles|thorny vegetation]]. Verreaux's eagle-owls mainly inhabit rather dry regions, some bordering semi-arid areas.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Wilson/> In central [[Mali]], for example, near the extreme northwestern limit of the species range, the habitat that hosts these owls averages less than {{convert|55|cm|in|abbr=on}} of rainfall annually.<ref name= Wilson/> However, they tend to require trees for both nesting and hunting purposes so do not occur in completely [[Open terrain|open habitats]] or true [[desert]]. Some degree of [[Ephemerality|ephemeral]] [[Wet season|rain]] to allow tree growth and survival is presumably required.<ref name= Cornell> Holt, D. W., R. Berkley, C. Deppe, P. L. Enríquez, J. L. Petersen, J. L. Rangel Salazar, K. P. Segars, K. L. Wood, and J. S. Marks (2023). Verreaux's Eagle-Owl (Ketupa lactea), version 1.2. In Birds of the World (N. D. Sly, Editor). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA.</ref><ref name= Maclean>Maclean, G. L. (1969). ''The breeding seasons of birds in the south-western Kalahari''. Ostrich, 40(S1), 179-192.</ref> They also range into [[Riparian forest|riverine forest]] adjacent to savanna and small, semi-open [[woodland]] surrounded by open country, though they are less likely to inhabit heavily wooded habitats.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Hume/> [[South Africa]]n eagle-owls are not infrequently found around [[floodplain]]s and [[marsh]]es, which may provide the primary nesting habitat in some areas.<ref>[http://sabap2.adu.org.za/ South Africa Bird Atlas Project 2 (January, 2010)]</ref> In [[Uganda]], they are largely associated with riparian woodlands.<ref>Seavy, N. E. (2004). ''Calling activity in Kibale National Park, Uganda''. ''Journal of Raptor Res.'', 38(3), 208-213.</ref><ref>Bowdler Sharpe, R. (1892). XLV.—On the ''Birds collected by Mr. FJ Jackson, FZS, during his recent Expedition to Uganda through the Territory of the Imperial British East‐African Company''. ''Ibis'', 34(4), 534-555.</ref> Verreaux's eagle-owl may live at nearly all elevations, from sea level to near the snow-line at around {{convert|3000|m|ft|abbr=on}} in elevation, such as in the [[Eastern Rift mountains]]. However, in general, they only sporadically inhabit [[rock (geology)|rocky]] areas and so are generally very scarce in mountainous regions.<ref name= Konig/> The [[bushveld]] of southern Africa is near ideal habitat for Verreaux's eagle-owl and the species may be found at near peak numbers here.<ref name= Hume/> The species was historically rare to absent from the [[Kalahari Desert]], but the introduction by man of invasive trees like [[conifer]]s, [[eucalyptus]] and [[acacia]]s, irrigation areas and prey species has allowed them to spottily occupy this region.<ref name= Hume/><ref name= Maclean/><ref>Herholdt, J. J. (1993). "Status of the Giant Eagle Owl ''Bubo lacteus'' in the Kalahari Gemsbok National Park, South Africa." ''Gabar'', 8, 17-20.</ref>

==Behavior==
[[File:Verreaux's Eagle Owl in Larsens Camp.jpg|thumb|Verreaux's eagle-owls spend daytime resting at a roost, which is almost always an ample tree branch]]
Verreaux's eagle-owls are [[nocturnal]] birds and roost by day in trees, with large, shaded horizontal branches of tall, old trees being preferred.<ref name= Konig/> In [[Kenya]], the most often-used perch trees were ''[[Croton megalocarpus]]'' and invasive ''[[Eucalyptus]]''.<ref name= Brown/> Elsewhere, ''[[Acacia]]'' trees may be used habitually.<ref name= Pitman>Pitman, C., & Adamson, J. (1978). "Notes on the ecology and ethology of the giant eagle owl, ''Bubo lacteus''." ''Honeyguide'', 95: 26-43.</ref> Despite normally choosing dense foliage to rest in, sometimes they may sit wherever their hunting path ends from the prior night, including relatively exposed perches.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Handbook/> They reportedly sleep rather lightly and will awaken very quickly to defend themselves from attack in daylight hours.<ref name= Handbook/> Family groups consisting of breeding pairs and their offspring frequently roost together and may engage in [[Social grooming|allopreening]] during this time.<ref name= Konig/> Reportedly some family groups include eagle-owls that had hatched up to three years prior, which if accurate is exceptional for any type of owl species.<ref name= Hume/><ref>Griesser, M., & Suzuki, T. N. (2016). ''Occasional cooperative breeding in birds and the robustness of comparative analyses concerning the evolution of cooperative breeding''. ''Zoological Letters'', 2(1), 7.</ref> During extremely hot days, this species may flutter its throat for cooling purposes and has been known to bathe in rain and shallow water during extreme heat in the middle of the afternoon but usually drinks when possible during nighttime.<ref name= Konig/> Each [[breeding pair]] of Verreaux's eagle-owl defends a territory and these may be extremely large, ranging in size up to {{convert|7000|ha|acre|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Hume/>

==Food and feeding==
[[File:Verreaux's eagle-owl, or giant eagle owl, Bubo lacteus eating a snake at Pafuri, Kruger National Park, South Africa (20675951922).jpg|thumb|left|An eagle-owl in [[Kruger National Park]] swallowing a snake]]
Verreaux's eagle-owl is considered an avian [[apex predator]], meaning it is at or near the top of the food chain and healthy adults normally have no natural predators. In many known aspects of its hunting behaviour, it is typical of the members of the genus ''Bubo''. This species hunts predominantly in early evening; however, they have been observed to swoop on prey during daylight.<ref name= Konig/> They usually fly to a different perch from their daytime roost to use as their habitual hunting perch.<ref name= Konig/> Verreaux's eagle-owls mainly hunt by gliding down on their prey from a perch. However, hunting on the wing has been reported, even of flying insects.<ref name= Brown/> On occasion, they hunt by flying low over a bush to catch prey by surprise or dash on the wing into dense foliage or through forests to catch a [[galago]] or other arboreal prey item.<ref name= Konig/> They will also sometimes run after prey on the ground, flapping their wings rapidly as they walk, or wade into shallow waters to pin down fish. The wing size of eagle-owls in general limits their flying speed and abilities in the open and so they require perches to execute most of their hunting behaviour.<ref name= Mikkola/><ref name= Steyn>Steyn, P. (1983). ''Birds of prey of southern Africa: Their identification and life histories''. Croom Helm, Beckenham (UK). 1983.</ref>

Even among the ''Bubo'' owls, most species of which are known to be highly opportunistic predators with indiscriminating diets, the Verreaux's eagle-owl is a particularly opportunistic predator. While earlier studies characterized [[great horned owl]], one of the most well-studied members of the genus ''Bubo'', as hunting whatever random species they first come across,<ref name= Errington>Errington, P.L. 1932. ''Studies on the Behavior of the Great Horned Owl''. ''Wilson Bulletin'', 12: 212-220.</ref> more modern dietary studies have contrarily shown their prey selection is not completely random and that regionally they selected [[cottontail]]s and [[hare]]s as prey instead of other foods regardless of prey population trends and became regional specialists on such prey, to such an extent that it predictably causes owl population declines at times when [[Leporidae|leporid]] numbers decline.<ref name= Marti>Marti, C. D. (1974). ''Feeding ecology of four sympatric owls''. ''Condor'', 45-61.</ref><ref name= Adamcik>Adamcik, R. S., A. W. Todd, and L. B. Keith. 1978. ''Demographic and dietary responses of Great Horned Owls during a snowshoe hare cycle''. ''Canadian Field-Naturalist'', 92:156-166.</ref> Furthermore, species-wide, great horned owls may select mammals as prey nearly 88% of the time.<ref name= Voous/> In contrast, studies have indicated that for the Verreaux's eagle-owl only around 56% of its diet is mammals and no single prey type predictably dominates their prey selection by biomass in multiple regions.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn/> To date, more than 100 prey species have been counted for this eagle-owl and, with only about half a dozen comprehensive dietary studies known to have been conducted, this probably only represents a small portion of the total prey selected.<ref name= Steyn/> Estimated prey size for the species has ranged from insects weighing less than {{convert|5|g|oz|abbr=on}} to [[ungulate]]s weighing at least {{convert|10|kg|lb|abbr=on}}. This is the second broadest size range positively attributed to a single owl species for prey items after the [[Eurasian eagle-owl]] and the largest exceptional upper prey-size also after the Eurasian species.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Andrews>Andrews, Peter (1990) ''Owls, Caves, and Fossils: Predation, Preservation, and Accumulation of Small Mammal Bones in Caves, with an Analysis of the Pleistocene Cave Faunas from Westbury-sub-Mendip, Somerset, UK'' University of Chicago Press. 231 pg.</ref><ref>Curry-Lindahl, K. ''Photographic Studies of Some Less Familiar Birds: LXXXIV. Eagle Owl''. British Birds, L: 486-490.</ref>

===Mammals===
The prey type most often associated with Verreaux's eagle-owl are [[hedgehog]]s. It appears that this species is the only routine predator of hedgehogs in Africa, most other predators of small-to-medium-sized mammals choosing to pursue other abundant mammals without the hedgehog's prickly defenses.<ref name= Kingdon>Kingdon, J., Happold, D., Butynski, T., Hoffmann, M., Happold, M., & Kalina, J. (2013). ''Mammals of Africa, Volume IV: Hedgehogs, Shrews and Bats''. A&C Black.</ref> In both the southernmost, from the western cape of [[South Africa]], and northernmost, a partial study of the foods at nests in central [[Mali]], food studies for this species have found hedgehogs to be the most significant contributor of biomass in Verreaux's eagle-owl nests.<ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/> The two known hedgehog prey species taken are the [[four-toed hedgehog]] (''Atelerix albiventris''), which averages {{convert|335|g|oz|abbr=on}} in adults,  in the north and the [[southern African hedgehog]] (''Atelerix frontalis''), which averages {{convert|350|g|oz|abbr=on}} in adults, in the south.<ref>Hallam, S. L. (2011). ''Heterothermy and seasonal patterns of metabolic rate in the southern African hedgehog (Atelerix frontalis)'' (Doctoral dissertation, MSc thesis, Nelson Mandela Metropolitan University, Port Elizabeth).</ref><ref>Santana, E. M., Jantz, H. E., & Best, T. L. (2010). ''Atelerix albiventris (Erinaceomorpha: Erinaceidae)''. ''Mammalian Species'', 42(1), 99-110.</ref> When capturing hedgehogs, the eagle-owl descends silently with its soft-comb wings and ambushes the hedgehog by imbedding its talons about the face. After death, the hedgehog is skinned of its prickly back before being consumed by either the eagle-owl itself or the young at the nest. This may result in over a dozen hedgehog skins being found around Verreaux's eagle-owl roosts near their nests.<ref name= Hume/><ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/><ref>Reeve, N. (1994). ''Hedgehogs''. London: T. & AD Poyser.</ref> The same method of dealing with hedgehogs is utilized by the [[Eurasian eagle-owl]], which is likewise reported as the only routine predator of hedgehogs in its native continent.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Wassink>Wassink, G. (2010). ''Het dieet van de Oehoe in Nederland en enkele aangrenzende gebieden in Duitsland''. Limosa. 83: 97-108.</ref> Studies in other areas have shown that, while hedgehogs are seemingly taken opportunistically, they are at best secondary as contributors of prey both in quantity and biomass.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Pitman/>

[[File:Hedgehogleft.jpg|thumb|left|A [[southern African hedgehog]], a common prey item for Verreaux's eagle-owls]]
In general, the diet of Verreaux's eagle-owl is seemingly random and highly variable. Eagle-owl species from temperate zones may have no choice but to predate [[rodent]]s which are rather small and this may require a nesting pair to capture up to a dozen rodents nightly.<ref name= Voous/><ref name= Marti2>Marti, C. D., Korpimäki, E., & Jaksić, F. M. (1993). ''Trophic structure of raptor communities: a three-continent comparison and synthesis''. In ''Current Ornithology'' (pp. 47-137). Springer US.</ref><ref name= Craighead>Craighead, J. J. and F. C. Craighead, Jr. 1956. ''Hawks, owls and wildlife''. Stackpole Co. Harrisburg, Pennsylvania.</ref> In comparison, the diversity and abundance of rodents is considerably greater in wild areas of sub-Saharan Africa and the Verreaux's eagle-owl seemingly ignores most small rodent species, with no rodent prey species known to average under {{convert|30|g|oz|abbr=on}} in adult body mass.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref name= Pitman/><ref name= Steyn/> In [[Kenya]], the most often recorded prey locally were ''[[Tachyoryctes]]'' [[Spalacidae|mole-rat]]s; however, these were recorded only slightly more often than other genera or species, including non-mammals.<ref name= Brown/> Several species of [[blesmol]], a separate family also sometimes referred to as mole-rats, have also been recorded as prey.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Pitman/><ref>Bennett, N. C., & Jarvis, J. U. (1988). ''The social structure and reproductive biology of colonies of the mole-rat, Cryptomys damarensis (Rodentia, Bathyergidae)''. ''Journal of Mammalogy'', 69(2), 293-302.</ref> Several [[Muridae|murid]] species have been hunted ranging in size from the {{convert|31|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[southern multimammate mouse]] (''Mastomys coucha'') to the two non-native ''[[Rattus]]'' species, including the {{convert|360|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[brown rat]] (''Rattus norvegicus'').<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref>Avery, D. M. (1985). ''The dispersal of brown rats Rattus norvegicus and new specimens from 19th century Cape Town''. ''Mammalia'', 49(4), 573-576.</ref><ref name= Happold>Happold, D. C., & Kingdon, J. (Eds.). (2013). ''Mammals of Africa. Volume 3, Rodents, hares and rabbits''. Bloomsbury.</ref><ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20090502064354/http://www.sensesofwildness.com/africa/2_5/05_02.HTM Dave Taylor's African Safari: Trophic Level IV: Large Carnivores – Verreaux's Eagle Owl – Page 2]. sensesofwildness.com</ref> Some larger rodents they've hunted have included the {{convert|529|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[cape ground squirrel]] (''Xerus inauris''), the {{convert|786|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[Gambian pouched rat]] (''Cricetomys qambianus'') and the {{convert|1900|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[lesser cane rat]] (''Thryonomys gregorianus'').<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Happold/><ref>Brain, C. K. (1983). ''The hunters or the hunted?: an introduction to African cave taphonomy''. University of Chicago Press.</ref> The largest known rodent prey is the [[South African springhare]] (''Pedetes capensis'') at an average adult weight of {{convert|3040|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Happold/> Avery, et al. (1985) opined that springhares may be only taken as carrion as they claim it be too large for the eagle-owl to overpower and indeed at least one South African springhare was fed on as roadkilled carrion. However, Avery, et al. (1985) also acknowledged that adult [[monkey]]s of larger size have verifiably been taken alive by the eagle-owls, so it certainly should not be ruled out that they also take live springhares.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/>

Many other mammals taken as prey by Verreaux's eagle-owl are seemingly any encountered except the much larger species, especially those that show a propensity for nocturnal or crepuscular activity. This species has hunted bats in several cases from the {{convert|8.1|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Lander's horseshoe bat]] (''Rhinolophus landeri''), the smallest known vertebrate prey species known for this eagle-owl, to ''[[Rousettus]]'' [[fruit bat]]s that weigh over {{convert|150|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref>Mikula, P., & Hromada, M. (2015). "Short Communication: True bats (''Microchiroptera'') in the diet of Verreaux's Eagle Owl ''Bubo lacteus''." ''Scopus'', 35(1), 50-51.</ref> Most other mammalian prey recorded or inferred as hunted by Verreaux's eagle-owl tend to be considerably larger. Both the [[scrub hare]] (''Lepus saxatilis'') and the [[cape hare]] (''Lepus capensis'') have been reported as food, the scrub species estimated to average {{convert|2,740|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} when taken.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/> In parts of [[Kenya]], the scrub hare can be a particularly significant contributor of biomass to the eagle-owl's diet.<ref name= Short>Short, L. L., & Horne, J. F. (2006). ''The Avifauna of an Upland Seasonal Woodland in Central Kenya: Ecology, Behavior, Breeding'' (No. 53). Zoologisches Forschungsmuseum Alexander Koenig.</ref> Other assorted mammalian prey species include the {{convert|540|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[golden-rumped elephant shrew]] (''Rhynchocyon chrysopygus'') and the {{convert|3800|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[cape hyrax]] (''Procavia capensis''), although it is possible that juvenile hyraxes are rather more commonly taken than adults.<ref>Rathbun, G. B. (1979). ''Rhynchocyon chrysopygus. VII. Interspecific Ecology''. ''Advances in Ethology'', 49: 51–54. doi: 10.1111/j.1439-0310.1979.tb00150.x.</ref><ref>Badenhorst, S., van Niekerk, K. L., & Henshilwood, C. S. (2014). ''Rock Hyraxes (Procavia capensis) from Middle Stone Age Levels at Blombos Cave, South Africa''. ''African Archaeological Review'', 31(1), 25-43.</ref><ref>Olds, M. & Soshani, J. (1982). ''Procavia capensis''. ''Mammalian Species'', 171: 1-7.</ref>

So far as is known, Verreaux's eagle-owl is the only living owl that preys upon multiple species of [[primate]], although isolated incidents of predation (normally on young primates) has been reported in two to three other large, tropical owls.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Voous/><ref name= Steyn/><ref>Hart, D. (2007). ''Predation on primates: a biogeographical analysis''. In ''Primate anti-predator strategies'' (pp. 27-59). Springer US.</ref> Multiple cases of predation against [[galago]]s have been reported, unsurprisingly as they represent all nocturnal primates in Africa, although they are seldom identified to species.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/> Known galago prey species have ranged from the {{convert|85.3|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Thomas's bushbaby]] (''Galagoides thomasi'') to the {{convert|1098|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[brown greater galago]] (''Otolemur crassicaudatus'').<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Butynski>T. M. Butynski, J. Kingdon, J. Kalina (eds.). 2013. ''Mammals of Africa. Volume II: Primates''. Bloomsbury Publishing, London, United Kingdom, 556 pp. {{ISBN|978-1-4081-2252-5}} (print).</ref> [[Old World monkey|Monkeys]] are also predated opportunistically. Particularly often reported in foods of the Verreaux's eagle-owl as primates go is the [[vervet monkey]] (''Chlorocebus pygerythrus'').<ref name= Butynski/><ref>Seyfarth, R., & Cheney, D. (1990). ''The assessment by vervet monkeys of their own and another species' alarm calls''. ''Animal Behaviour'', 40(4), 754-764.</ref><ref>Struhsaker, T. T. (1967). ''Ecology of vervet monkeys (Cercopithecus aethiops) in the Masai-Amboseli game reserve, Kenya''. ''Ecology'', 892-904.</ref> Incidents of successful predation have included vervets that were half-grown, which the eagle-owl was able to fly off with (despite being about as heavy as the eagle-owl itself), and an adult vervet of an estimated weight of {{convert|4000|to|5000|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, which an eagle-owl took on the ground and subsequently dismembered.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Pitman/><ref name= Steyn/> However, considering the formidable gauntlet of predators that vervet monkeys face, the Verreaux's eagle-owl is one of its more minor predators and attacks on them may be considered incidental, due in part to the monkey's primarily diurnal activities.<ref>Seyfarth, R. M., Cheney, D. L., & Marler, P. (1980). Vervet monkey alarm calls: semantic communication in a free-ranging primate. ''Animal Behaviour'', 28(4), 1070–1094.</ref><ref>Pasternak, G. M. (2011). ''Environmental effects on group structure and vigilance in vervet monkeys'' (Doctoral dissertation, Lethbridge, Alta.: University of Lethbridge, Dept. of Psychology, c2011).</ref><ref>Josephs, N. (2015). ''Social and spatial structure of vervet monkey troops'' (Doctoral dissertation, Lethbridge, Alta.: University of Lethbridge, Dept. of Psychology).</ref><ref>Ducheminsky, N., Henzi, S. P., & Barrett, L. (2014). ''Responses of vervet monkeys in large troops to terrestrial and aerial predator alarm calls''. ''Behavioral Ecology'', 25(6), 1474–1484.</ref> Other monkey species believed to be occasionally vulnerable to attacks include the [[blue monkey]] (''Cercopithecus mitis''), which is similar in size to the vervet, [[patas monkey]]s (''Erythrocebus patas'') and the young of the [[chacma baboon]] (''Papio ursinus''). Adult patas monkeys, averaging some {{convert|8633|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, can be even larger than vervet monkeys but whether they take prime adults of the species is questionable.<ref name= Butynski/><ref>Coleman, B. (2013). ''Spatial and temporal determinants of samango monkey (Cercopithecus mitis erythrarchus) resource acquisition and predation avoidance behaviour'' (Doctoral dissertation, Durham University).</ref><ref>''Comparison of play behaviour of four guenon species: Diana monkey (Cercopithecus diana), de Brazza monkey (Cercopithecus neglectus), Patas monkey (Erythrocebus patas) and Vervet (Chlorocebus pygerythus) with regard to self-handicapping''; diploma thesis [in English] – 57 pp. Faculty of Natural Sciences, University of South Bohemia, České Budějovice, Czech Republic.</ref><ref>MacLean, G. (1969) ''Proceedings of the Third Pan-African Ornithological Congress held at Pretoriuskop, Kruger National Park''. pp. 182.</ref>

There are a few verified cases of Verreaux's eagle-owls feeding on [[ungulate]]s; however, some authors such as Avery, et al. (1985) feel that these generally represent cases of scavenging on carrion. The remains of an adult [[Cape grysbok|grysbok]] (''Raphicerus melanotis''), weighing an estimated {{convert|10670|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, was opined with certainty to have been taken as carrion per this study.<ref name= Avery/> Steyn (1982) accepted that this species could take live prey weighing up to {{convert|10000|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} on rare occasions; however, he stated in a case of an adult [[common duiker]] (''Sylvicapra grimmia'') being fed on by an eagle-owl that the duiker was likely roadkill.<ref name= Steyn/> Scavenging on carrion is generally a rare behaviour in owls and has been reported in only a few cases where large owls are exceptionally hungry.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn2/> Live ungulates verified to have been hunted have included piglets of [[common warthog]]s (''Phacochoerus africanus''), which have an average birth weight of only {{convert|665|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} but grow to over {{convert|2000|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} in just a couple weeks.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref>''Common Warthog (Phacochoerus africanus) Fact Sheet, 2015''. c2015. San Diego (CA): San Diego Zoo Global; [accessed 2016]. http://ielc.libguides.com/sdzg/factsheets/warthog_com{{Dead link|date=January 2026 |bot=InternetArchiveBot }}.</ref> Adult [[Kirk's dik-dik]]s (''Madoqua kirkii''), one of the smallest [[antelope]] species at an average of {{convert|4590|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} have also been hunted by Verreaux's eagle-owl.<ref>Kingswood, S. C., & Kumamoto, A. T. (1997). ''Madoqua kirkii''. ''Mammalian Species Archive'', 569, 1-10.</ref><ref>''Madoqua'', Volume 19, Issue 2 South-west Africa. Nature Conservation and Tourism of the South West Africa Administration., 1997 Cornell University.</ref>

Among [[Carnivora|mammalian carnivores]] the bulk of predatory incidents have reportedly involved [[mongoose]]s. Common, social species from savanna-edge such as the {{convert|710|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[yellow mongoose]] (''Cynictis penicillata'') and the {{convert|725|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[meerkat]]s (''Suricata suricatta'') have been attacked, as well as larger, shy forest dwellers such as the {{convert|2500|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[Jackson's mongoose]] (''Bdeogale jacksoni'').<ref name= Hoffman>J. Kingdon, M. Hoffmann (eds.). 2013. ''Mammals of Africa. Volume V: Carnivores, Pangolins, Equids and Rhinoceroses''. Bloomsbury Publishing, London, United Kingdom, 544 pp. {{ISBN|978-1-4081-2255-6}}</ref><ref>Taylor, P.J. & Meester, J. (1993). ''Cynictis penicillata''. ''Mammal Species'', 432: 1-7.</ref> An adult [[Meller's mongoose]] (''Rhynchogale melleri'') weighing about {{convert|2200|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} which was taken by a Verreaux's eagle-owl on the wing represents the second heaviest known object successfully flown with this species after the aforementioned half-grown vervet monkey.<ref name= Steyn2>Steyn, P. (2010). ''A delight of owls: African owls observed''. Jacana Media.</ref> Other smallish carnivores known to fall prey to Verreaux's eagle-owls include the {{convert|292|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[African striped weasel]] (''Poecilogale albinucha'') and its larger cousin, the {{convert|817|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[striped polecat]] (''Ictonyx striatus''), which in one nest from the border of the [[Kalahari]] represented the sole prey species for a pair of eagle-owls.<ref name= Avery/><ref>Larivière, S. (2001). ''Poecilogale albinucha''. ''Mammalian Species'', 681(1), 1-4.</ref><ref>Larivière, S. (2002). ''Ictonyx striatus''. ''Mammalian Species'', 1-5.</ref><ref>Rasa, O.A.E. (2007). ''Diet of a Verreaux's Eagle-Owl in the Kalahari''. ''Gabar'', 18: 1.</ref> In southern Africa, both the [[cape genet]] (''Genetta tigrina''), averaging {{convert|1732|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, and the {{convert|1600|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[black-footed cat]] (''Felis nigripes''), the smallest felid in Africa, have been included amongst their prey.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://cannundrum.blogspot.com/2014_06_01_archive.html |title=Verreaux's eagle-owl |publisher=Cannudrum |access-date=4 May 2016}}</ref><ref>Stuart, C. T. (1981). ''Notes on the mammalian carnivores of the Cape Province, South Africa''. ''Bontebok'' 1: 1–58.</ref><ref>Renard, A., Lavoie, M., Pitt, J. A., & Larivière, S. (2015). ''Felis nigripes (Carnivora: Felidae)''. ''Mammalian Species'', 47(925), 78-83.</ref><ref>Olbricht, G., & Sliwa, A. (1997). ''In situ and ex situ observations and management of Black‐footed cats Felis nigripes''. ''International Zoo Yearbook'', 35(1), 81-89.</ref> The Verreaux's eagle-owl is thought to be a threat to even larger carnivores, including the {{convert|4150|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[bat-eared fox]] (''Otocyon megalotis'') and the {{convert|10000|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[aardwolf]] (''Proteles cristata''), although whether healthy adults of the latter are in danger is doubtful.<ref>Clark Jr, H. O. (2005). ''Otocyon megalotis''. ''Mammalian Species'', 1-5.</ref><ref>''Bat-eared fox, Otocyon megalotis'', J.A.J. Nel and B. Maas, Sillero-Zubiri, C., Hoffmann, M. and Macdonald, D.W. (eds). 2004. ''Canids: Foxes, Wolves, Jackals and Dogs''. Status Survey and Conservation Action Plan. IUCN/SSC Canid Specialist Group. Gland, Switzerland and Cambridge, UK. x + 430 pp.</ref><ref>Welch, R. J., Périquet, S., Petelle, M. B., & le Roux, A. (2017). ''Hunter or hunted? Perceptions of risk and reward in a small mesopredator''. ''Journal of Mammalogy'', 98(6), 1531-1537.</ref><ref>Sliwa, A. (1996). ''[http://citeseerx.ist.psu.edu/viewdoc/download?doi=10.1.1.603.4798&rep=rep1&type=pdf A functional analysis of scent marking and mating behaviour in the aardwolf]'' (Doctoral dissertation, University of Pretoria).</ref><ref>Kingdon, J. (2015). ''The Kingdon field guide to African mammals''. Bloomsbury Publishing.</ref> A scientifically-observed attack on an adult male [[African wildcat]] (''Felis silvestris cafra''), which can weigh more than about {{convert|4500|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, was aborted after the eagle-owl apparently deemed that the felid was too heavy to take flight with.<ref>Sliwa, Alexander, Marna Herbst, and M. Mills. "Black-footed cats (Felis nigripes) and African wild cats (Felis silvestris): a comparison of two small felids from South African arid lands." Biology and conservation of wild felids (DW Macdonald and AJ Loveridge, eds.). Oxford University Press, Oxford, United Kingdom (2010): 537-558.</ref> However, domesticated cats of any size may fall prey to Verreaux's eagle-owl. At [[Lake Baringo|Lake Baringo Country Club]] in [[Kenya]], this eagle-owl has apparently taken to habitually hunting outdoor cats, reportedly making the cats on the grounds highly skittish.<ref name= Harris>{{cite web |url=http://ecoport.org/ep?SearchType=pdb&PdbID=31544 |title=''Bubo lacteus'' |author=Paul |author2=Helen Harris |author3=Kenya Birds |name-list-style=amp |publisher=Ecoport |access-date=4 May 2016}}</ref>

===Birds===
[[File:Black-headed Heron flying RWD.jpg|thumb|A South African study showed the [[black-headed heron]] as the most important prey by biomass for Verreaux's eagle-owls.]]
Verreaux's eagle-owl takes a diverse range of birds as prey. More than 50 avian prey species have been identified and they may locally exceed mammals in importance in the diet, somewhat unusually for eagle-owls.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Voous/> No one type of bird can be said to be predictably favored as prey and any avian species unfortunate enough to have a nighttime roost or nest that happens to be in an eagle-owl's foraging path may fall victim to this species. Many cases of predation involve nest robbery, with nestlings or fledglings being taken, although adult birds may be taken just as often, especially for species with less conspicuous nests.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/> In [[South Africa]]'s [[De Hoop Nature Reserve]], it was found that birds were somewhat better represented by both number, 43.3% of remains, and biomass, 57.84% of remains, than mammals or any other prey group. The species best represented in biomass in the prior study was the [[black-headed heron]] (''Ardea melanocephala'') with several adults estimated to average {{convert|1260|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} being found among the prey remains.<ref name= Avery/> Other fairly common, largish herons are also known to fall prey at night to Verreaux's eagle-owl including the {{convert|873|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[great egret|common egret]] (''Ardea alba''), the {{convert|1443|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[grey heron]] (''Ardea cinerea'') and the {{convert|975|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[purple heron]] (''Ardea purpurea'').<ref name = "CRC"/><ref name= Harris/><ref>Tomlinson, D. N. S. (1974). ''Studies of the Purple Heron, Part 1: Heronry structure, nesting habits and reproductive success''. ''Ostrich'', 45(3), 175-181.</ref>

Other medium-sized [[water bird]]s known to have been represented in this species diet include the {{convert|1008|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[yellow-billed duck]] (''Anas undulata''), the {{convert|983|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[African black duck]] (''Anas sparsa''), the {{convert|596|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[African swamphen]] (''Porphyrio madagascariensis'') and the {{convert|825|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[red-knobbed coot]] (''Fulica cristata'').<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref name= Hancock>Hancock, P., & Weiersbye, I. (2015). ''Birds of Botswana''. Princeton University Press.</ref> Besides herons, another well-represented group of birds in the diet are [[Galliformes|galliforms]]. Perhaps the most widely preyed species reported from this group is the {{convert|1229|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[helmeted guineafowl]] (''Numida meleagris''), which may seasonally dominate the eagle-owl's food in [[Kenya]].<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Short/> More modestly sized wild galliform species reported in the diet including the {{convert|96|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[common quail]] (''Coturnix coturnix'') and the {{convert|390|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[grey-winged francolin]] (''Francolinus africanus'').<ref name= Avery/><ref name = "CRC"/> Domestic fowl, especially those allowed back to a semi-feral state and thus sleeping in the open as is prevalent in Africa, are taken when encountered, including [[chicken]]s and [[peafowl]]s.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Chittenden>Chittenden, H. (2014). "Prey items of Verreaux's Eagle-Owl ''Bubo lacteus'' breeding in suburbia." ''Gabar'' 25: 15–16.</ref>

Various upland birds recorded as prey include the {{convert|177|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Namaqua sandgrouse]] (''Pterocles namaqua''), the {{convert|350|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[rock pigeon]] (''Columba livia''), the {{convert|84|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[laughing dove]] (''Streptopelia senegalensis''), the {{convert|169|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[Senegal coucal]] (''Centropus senegalensis''), the {{convert|49|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[scaly-throated honeyguide]] (''Indicator variegatus'') and several species of [[hornbill]], ranging in size from the {{convert|139|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[northern red-billed hornbill]] (''Tockus erythrorhynchus'') to the {{convert|1235|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[silvery-cheeked hornbill]] (''Bycanistes brevis'').<ref name= Avery/><ref name= "CRC"/><ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref>Kemp, A. C. (1976). ''A study of the ecology, behaviour and systematics of Tockus hornbills:(Aves: Bucerotidae)'' (No. 20). Transvaal Museum.</ref><ref>Moreau, R. E. (1936). ''The breeding biology of certain East African hornbills (Bucerotidae)''. ''Jour. East Africa and Uganda Nat. Hist. Soc'', 13, 1-28.</ref> Among [[passerine]]s, the most frequently taken are likely to be [[corvid]]s, which are often favored by ''Bubo'' owls from around the world due to their large size, relatively open nests and frequently easy-to-find, communal nocturnal roosts. To date the [[cape crow]] (''Corvus capensis'') and [[pied crow]] (''Corvus albus'') are the corvids reported in dietary studies. In [[Ethiopia]], [[thick-billed raven]]s (''Corvus crassirostris''), which at {{convert|1500|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} are possibly the heaviest corvid species in the world, mobbed them vigorously and seemed to consider them a primary threat.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref>de Castro, J. J., & de Castro, M. (2014). "Verreaux's Eagle Owl ''Bubo lacteus'' attacked by thick-billed ravens ''Corvus crassirostris''." ''Scopus'', 32(1), 51-52.</ref> Smaller passerines are by no means ignored.<ref>Thiollay, J.-M. ''Natural predation on quelea.'' Quelea quelea. Africa's Bird Pest (1989): 216-229.</ref><ref>Raihani, N. J., Nelson‐Flower, M. J., Moyes, K., Browning, L. E., & Ridley, A. R. (2010). ''Synchronous provisioning increases brood survival in cooperatively breeding pied babblers''. ''Journal of Animal Ecology'', 79(1), 44-52.</ref> [[White-eye]]s are among the more frequently taken smaller passerines, with the {{convert|10.9|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[southern yellow white-eye]] (''Zosterops anderssoni'') being the smallest identified avian prey species, although [[Anthoscopus|penduline tit]]s (''Anthoscopus'' ssp.) are likely to be even smaller.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name=Diversity>[http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/strigidae/bubo_lacteus.htm Bubo lacteus (Verreaux's eagle-owl, Giant eagle owl)] {{webarchive |url=https://web.archive.org/web/20111209022344/http://www.biodiversityexplorer.org/birds/strigidae/bubo_lacteus.htm |date=December 9, 2011 }}. Biodiversityexplorer.org. Retrieved on 2012-08-24.</ref> The largest bird to be hunted by Verreaux's eagle-owl is complicated by the fact that they often take relatively small nestlings of larger species, such as [[ostrich]]es (''Struthio camelus'') and [[grey crowned crane]]s (''Balearica regulorum'').<ref name= Steyn/> The only avian prey items successfully attacked larger than other types of [[birds of prey]] (reviewed later) are likely [[bustard]]s.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/> Most predation records have reported on relatively small bustards, namely [[Northern black korhaan|northern]] (''Afrotis afraoides'') and [[southern black korhaan]]s (''Afrotis afra''), which average only {{convert|745|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} and {{convert|690|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, respectively.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Hockey>Hockey PAR, Dean WRJ and Ryan PG (eds) 2005. ''Roberts – Birds of southern Africa, VIIth ed.'' The Trustees of the John Voelcker Bird Book Fund, Cape Town.</ref> Larger species of [[bustard]] thought to be threatened by Verreaux's eagle-owl are the {{convert|4790|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[Denham's bustard]] (''Neotis denhami'') and the {{convert|8430|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[kori bustard]] (''Ardeotis kori''), although it is not clear whether adults (especially males) are attacked in the latter species.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Hockey/><ref>Piersma, T., del Hoyo, J., Elliot, A., & Sangatal, J. (1996). ''Handbook of the birds of the world: Hoatzin to Auks''</ref>

===Other prey===
[[File:Verreaux' s Eagle Owl (Bubo lacteus) (5984944418).jpg|thumb|A Verreaux's eagle-owl near a flying moth, which can be as likely prey as a much larger item]]
[[Reptile]]s and [[amphibian]]s are occasional prey for Verreaux's eagle-owls. Various snakes have been included in their diet ranging from the small, innocuous [[Boaedon|brown house snake]] (''Boaedon fuliginosus'') at {{convert|31|g|oz|abbr=on}} to large and venomous [[Egyptian cobra]]s (''Naja haje'') weighing over {{convert|454|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref>Feldman, A., & Meiri, S. (2013). ''Length–mass allometry in snakes''. ''Biological Journal of the Linnean Society'', 108(1), 161-172.</ref> [[Frog]]s were amongst the prominent prey recorded for suburban-breeding eagle-owls in South Africa, namely the [[African red toad]] (''Schismaderma carens'') and the [[guttural toad]] (''Amietophrynus gutturalis''). Unidentified frogs were fairly significant in the diet from [[Kenya]].<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Chittenden/><ref>Vawda, A., Burger, F. J., & Smit, A. L. (1981). ''Glucose tolerance in the toad Bufo gutturalis (Power)''. ''South African Journal of Zoology'', 16(3), 156-162.</ref> The largest herpetological prey known is the [[Nile monitor]] (''Varanus niloticus''), at a mean mature mass of {{convert|5850|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}, these primarily diurnal reptiles can provide a fulfilling meal but can be hard to subdue even if ambushed unaware.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref>Condon, K. (1987). ''A kinematic analysis of mesokinesis in the Nile monitor (Varanus niloticus)''. Experimental biology, 47(2), 73.</ref><ref>Bartlett, R., P. Bartlett. 1996. ''Monitors, Tegus, and Related Lizards''. Hauppauge, New York: Barron's Educational Series, Inc.</ref>

Predation on fish has been reported but no fish have been observed firsthand in dietary studies.<ref name= Steyn/> A surprisingly wide range of [[invertebrates]] have been reported in the diet for this species. In some cases, they may prey on insects as small as [[termite]]s and even smaller invertebrates have been recorded in pellets such as [[Oribatida|oribatid mite]]s and ''[[Sarcophaga]]'' flies, but are likely consumed incidentally while eating a larger item, either from carrion or the stomach of the prey itself.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Vernon>Vernon, C.J. 1980. ''Prey of six species of owl at the Zimbabwe Ruins – 1970-1975''. ''Honeyguide'' 101: 26-28.</ref> Unidentified [[scorpion]]s, [[spider]]s and [[millipede]]s have also been reported in their foods.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn/> Most attacks on insects involve large [[ground beetle]]s or [[dung beetle]]s. Verreaux's eagle-owl has been known to feed on dung beetles among herds of [[African buffalo]] (''Syncerus caffer'') by night, boldly diving below the massive bovids' legs, and will readily feed on beetles among [[elephant]] dung when available.<ref name= Avery/><ref>Pretorius, M. & Wolfaardt, V. (2014). "Verreaux's Eagle-Owl ''Bubo lacteus'' feeding on dung beetles." ''Gabar'', 25 (2): 69-70.</ref><ref>Friedmann, H. & Stager, K. E. (1967) "Results of the 1966 Cheney expedition to the Samburu district, Kenya." Los Angeles County Museum of Natural History.</ref>

===Interspecies predatory relations===

Sub-Saharan Africa has many species of owl, although there is less species diversity than in some areas of similar latitude in the [[neotropic]]s and south Asia.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Mikkola/> It also hosts the most species of [[Horned owl|eagle-owl]] with approximately eight  species of ''Bubo'' and ''[[Ketupa]]'' species collectively that are referred to as eagle-owl and all three [[fishing owl]] species as well.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/> Due to the diversity here, there are a number of distinctions between habitat preference, primary prey types and body size among the so-called eagle-owls of Africa.<ref name= Andrews/><ref name= Steyn2/> The smallest species of the ''Ketupa'' genus reside solely in Africa, the [[akun eagle-owl]] (''Ketupa leucosticta'') as well as the smallest ''Bubo'' the [[greyish eagle-owl]] (''Bubo cinerascens'') and the [[spotted eagle-owl]] (''Bubo africanus''), in rough order of increasing size. These three species are all primarily insectivores and are much reduced in the size and strength of their feet and talons compared to most other contemporary species, although the spotted eagle-owl can be locally specialized to feed on small [[rodent]]s as well.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Mikkola/><ref name= Weick/><ref>Benson, C. W. (1962). "The food of the Spotted Eagle-owl ''Bubo africanus''." ''Ostrich'', 33(4).</ref> While the akun is a primary forest-dweller as are the medium-sized [[Fraser's eagle-owl|Fraser's]] and [[Usambara eagle-owl]] and large [[Shelley's eagle-owl]] and thus is not likely to co-exist with Verreaux's eagle-owls except in rare cases, the northerly-distributed greyish eagle-owl (which was at one point considered merely a subspecies of the spotted) and the southerly-distributed spotted eagle-owl have much more similar habitat preferences to the Verreaux's species.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/><ref>Mendelsohn, J. M. (1989). "Habitat preferences, population size, food and breeding of six owl species in the Springbok Flats, South Africa." ''Ostrich'', 60(4), 183-190.</ref><ref>Riegert, J., Sedláček, O., & Hutterer, R. (2008). "Diet of sympatric African grass owl (''Tyto capensis'') and spotted eagle owl (''Bubo africanus'') in the Bamenda Highlands, NW Cameroon." ''African Journal of Ecology'', 46(3), 428-431.</ref> Of the non-piscivorous owls in Africa, the [[Cape eagle-owl]] can have a somewhat broad diet and a capability to take large prey but is more specialized to feed on a narrow range of mammals, [[mole-rat]]s often supplemented with [[rock hyrax]], than the Verreaux's eagle-owl.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref>Ogada, D. L., & Kibuthu, P. M. (2009). "Impacts of agriculture on the diet and productivity of Mackinder's Eagle Owls (''Bubo capensis mackinderi'') in Kenya." ''Biotropica'', 41(4), 485-492.</ref> The Cape eagle-owl has a fairly strong preference for nesting and hunting within the confines of rocky and mountainous habitats, whereas the Verreaux's is at best sporadic in such areas.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Voous2>Voous, K. H. (1966). "The distribution of owls in Africa in relation to general zoogeographical problems." ''Ostrich'', 37(S1), 499-506.</ref><ref>Ogada, D. L., & Kibuthu, P. M. (2012). "Breeding ecology of Mackinder's Eagle-Owls (''Bubo capensis mackinderi'') in farmlands of central Kenya." ''Journal of Raptor Research'', 46(4), 327-335.</ref><ref>Jackson, H. D. (1973). "The Cape eagle owl ''Bubo capensis'' in Mozambique." ''Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club'', 93(10).</ref> In east Africa and South Africa, habitat degradation has allowed the more adaptable Verreaux's eagle-owl to move into areas inhabited by cape eagle-owls and has presented the possible issue of the Verreaux's competitively excluding the smaller species.<ref name= Voous2/><ref>Ogada, D. L., & Kibuthu, P. M. (2008). "Conserving Mackinder's eagle owls in farmlands of Kenya: assessing the influence of pesticide use, tourism and local knowledge of owl habits in protecting a culturally loathed species." ''Environmental Conservation'', 35(03), 252-260.</ref>

[[File:Grass Owl adult.jpg|thumb|left|Other owls such as [[African grass owl]]s may fall victim to predation by Verreaux's eagle-owls.]]
Outside of the genus ''Bubo'' and the sister genera ''Ketupa'' and ''Scotopelia'', other owls in Africa are much smaller than Verreaux's eagle-owls and are more likely to be viewed as prey than competition. Among the small-to-mid-sized owls that have fallen prey to this species are the [[Western barn owl|barn owl]] (''Tyto alba'') and the [[African grass owl]] (''Tyto capensis''), both of which average around {{convert|419|g|oz|abbr=on}} in body mass in Africa, the {{convert|334|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[marsh owl]] (''Asio capensis'') and the {{convert|216|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[southern white-faced owl]] (''Ptilopsis granti'').<ref name= Brown/><ref name = "CRC"/><ref name= Voous/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Harris/> The only verified interactions with other typical eagle-owls have been predatory, as the {{convert|645|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[spotted eagle-owl]] has been recorded among their prey in a few cases.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn/> There are several owls with broadly similar habitat preferences from [[African scops owl]]s (''Otus senegalensis'') to [[African wood owl]]s (''Strix woodfordii'') that have not been reported as food but are almost certainly occasionally threatened by Verreaux's eagle-owls.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/> As is commonly the case with eagle-owls, the Verreaux's eagle-owl is perhaps the most serious predatory threat to diurnal raptors in its range, most often ambushing raptors on their prominent nests upon nightfall and freely killing birds of prey of any age from nestlings to adults. Such prey is not quantitatively significant as a food source but since raptors as a rule are sparsely distributed the habitual visitation of a single or pair of Verreaux's eagle-owl can potentially be devastating to a local population.<ref name= Avery/><ref name= Voous/><ref name= Steyn/> Among the species of small-to-medium-sized raptors known to be attacked are the {{convert|638|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[African harrier-hawk]] (''Polyboroides typus''), the {{convert|675|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[pale chanting goshawk]] (''Melierax canorus''), the {{convert|507|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[African marsh harrier]] (''Circus ranivorus''), the {{convert|110|g|oz|abbr=on}} [[scissor-tailed kite]] (''Chelictinia riocourii''), the {{convert|291.5|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[African goshawk]] (''Accipiter tachiro'') the {{convert|875|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[common buzzard]] (''Buteo buteo'') and the {{convert|640|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[Wahlberg's eagle]] (''Hieraaetus wahlbergi'').<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Avery/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Fry>Fry, C. H., Keith, S. & Urban, E. K. 1988. ''The Birds of Africa Vol. 3''. Academic Press, London.</ref>

There are reports of Verreaux's eagle-owls attacking even larger raptorial birds. A case of the Verreaux's eagle-owl killing an adult [[Pel's fishing owl]] in [[Botswana]] was verified. At roughly {{convert|2000|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} in body mass, the fishing owl is of nearly the same size as the eagle-owl.<ref>Wright, D., & Hancock, P. (2008). ''Pel's Fishing-Owl Scotopelia peli falls prey to Verreaux's Eagle-Owk Bubo lacteus in the Okavango Delta''. ''Gabar'', 19(2), 73-74.</ref> Cases where they've attacked the nests of particularly large diurnal birds of prey have sometimes involved only nestlings being victimized, such as attacks on the [[hooded vulture]] (''Necrosyrtes monachus'') and the [[bateleur]] (''Terathopius ecaudatus''); none of the adults, which are about the same average adult body mass as the Verreaux's eagle-owls, have been reported as prey.<ref name= Steyn/><ref>Watson, R. T. (1988). "The influence of nestling predation on nest site selection and behaviour of the bateleur." ''South African Journal of Zoology'', 23(3), 143-149.</ref> However, in some even larger birds of prey, adults as well as nestlings and fledglings have been killed. Successful nighttime attacks have been reported on adults of the {{convert|2810|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[African fish eagle]] (''Haliaeetus vocifer'') and the {{convert|4017|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[secretarybird]] (''Sagittarius serpentarius'').<ref name= Steyn/> In the [[Matobo National Park|Matobo Hills]] of [[Zimbabwe]], the Verreaux's eagle-owl has been considered as one of the inferred predators of {{convert|4195|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} [[Verreaux's eagle]] (''Aquila verreauxii''), although whether adults or only nestlings are vulnerable is not definitely clear.<ref>Gargett, V. (1969). "Black Eagle survey, Rhodes Matopos National Park: a population study, 1964–1968." ''Ostrich'', 40(S1), 397-414.</ref><ref>Cottrell, J.A. (1970). ''Black eagle fly free''. Cornell University (Purnell).</ref>

Other than these rare cases, larger birds of prey such as eagles are not usually harassed by Verreaux's eagle-owl and are more aptly viewed as competitors. In fact, the [[martial eagle]] (''Polemaetus bellicosus'') is sometimes regarded as the diurnal ecological equivalent of the Verreaux's eagle-owl.<ref name= Hancock/> The martial eagle has rather similar habitat preferences to the eagle-owl and has a similarly broad, opportunistic diet.<ref name= Steyn/><ref>Boshoff, A. F., & Palmer, N. G. (1980). "Macro-analysis of prey remains from martial eagle nests in the Cape Province." ''Ostrich'', 51(1), 7-13.</ref><ref>Boshoff, A. F., Palmer, N. G., & Avery, G. (1990). "Regional variation in the diet of martial eagles in the Cape Province, South Africa." ''S. Afr. J. Wildl. Res'', 20, 57-68.</ref> At roughly {{convert|4200|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} in average body mass, the martial eagle is roughly twice as heavy as Verreaux's eagle-owl and takes correspondingly large prey, its average prey weight range being {{convert|1000|to|5000|g|lb|1|abbr=on}} and the eagles are capable of exceptionally taking prey up to nearly nine times their own weight, whereas most of the eagle-owls prey does not exceed {{convert|1000|to|1500|g|lb|1|abbr=on}}. Verreaux's eagle-owl is likely to give martial eagles a respectful space during daytime and there are no records of the two species harassing one another.<ref name= Avery/><ref name = "CRC"/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Raptors>Ferguson-Lees, J., & Christie, D. A. (2001). ''Raptors of the World''. Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.</ref> Another particularly large and aggressive eagle, the [[crowned eagle]] (''Stephanoaetus coronatus''), is largely a forest-dweller and so is less directly a diurnal equivalent. There is a single recorded instance of an immature crowned eagle being aggressively displaced at night by an adult Verreaux's eagle-owl when it happened to encroach on the eagle-owl's territory but without bloodshed and eagle-owls would do well to avoid the exceptionally powerful eagle.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Raptors/><ref>Brown, L. H. (1982). "The prey of the crowned eagle ''Stephanoaetus coronatus'' in Central Kenya." ''Scopus'', 6, 91-94.</ref> Taken together, the Verreaux's, the Shelley's and the Cape eagle-owls could be seen as nocturnally replacing the eagle species of martial, crowned and Verreaux's eagles in the respective habitats of savanna, forest and rocky areas but their increasingly diminishing size in comparison to the diurnal eagles means that, generally speaking, less large-bodied prey is likely to be attacked.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Steyn2/><ref name= Raptors/><ref>Newton, I. (2010). ''Population ecology of raptors''. A&C Black.</ref>  Despite its place near the top of the nocturnal avian food chain, in 2013 a remote wildlife camera videotaped a [[black-backed jackal]] (''Canis mesomelas'') attacking and killing a Verreaux's eagle owl at a watering hole.<ref name= Jackal>{{cite web |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=rkpq_sL0VOI |archive-url=https://ghostarchive.org/varchive/youtube/20211219/rkpq_sL0VOI |archive-date=2021-12-19 |url-status=live|title=GRAPHIC: Jackal Kills Giant Eagle Owl at Pete's Pond 7-11-2013 |date=10 July 2013 |publisher=YouTube |access-date=2014-12-02}}{{cbignore}}</ref> Similar rare successful attacks on [[great horned owl]]s and [[Eurasian eagle-owl]]s by smaller [[red fox]]es  (''Vulpes vulpes'') have been reported, but in these cases the horned owl was mysteriously grounded and the eagle-owl was nesting in too-easily accessed sea cliffs. More often foxes are prey rather than predators for northern ''Bubo'' owls.<ref>Mils, C.T. ''A Great Horned Owl killed by a Red Fox''. Wisconsin Department of Natural Resources, pp. 158.</ref><ref>Jaume, S. (2000). "Depredaciones de Zorro ''Vulpes vulpes'' sobre Búho Real Bubo bubo en un área del litoral Ibérico." ''Ardeola'', 47(1), 97-99.</ref><ref name= Jaksic>Jaksić, F. M., & Marti, C. D. (1984). "Comparative food habits of ''Bubo'' owls in Mediterranean-type ecosystems." ''Condor'', 288-296.</ref><ref name= Leditznig>Leditznig, C., Leditznig, W., & Gossow, H. (2001). "15 Jahre Untersuchungen am Uhu (''Bubo bubo'') im Mostviertel Niederösterreichs-Stand und Entwicklungstendenzen." ''Egretta'', 44: 45-73.</ref><ref>Storm, G. L., Andrews, R. D., Phillips, R. L., Bishop, R. A., Siniff, D. B., & Tester, J. R. (1976). "Morphology, reproduction, dispersal, and mortality of midwestern red fox populations." ''Wildlife Monographs'', 3-82.</ref> Given that the Verreaux's eagle-owl is surprisingly bold about coming to their ground to, among other things capture beetles, feed on prey too large to carry in flight or, as is likely the case in the jackal attack, drink water, it is possible that the jackal was simply able to ambush an incautious eagle-owl rather than a grounded one.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Jackal/> Perhaps even more unexpectedly, an adult [[bateleur]] was filmed killing a Verreaux's eagle-owl by day, though whether this was predation or a competitive or anti-predatory attack is unclear.<ref>{{cite web |first1=Mickey|last1=SA|title=Bateleur kills a Giant Eagle Owl in Kruger National Park |url=https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WNqk8-BEbek|website=youtube.com |date=9 December 2018 |publisher=YouTube|access-date=2021-07-24}}</ref>

==Breeding==
[[File:Verreaux's Eagle-Owl (Bubo lacteus) - Flickr - Lip Kee (1).jpg|thumb|A Verreaux's eagle-owl is disturbed during the day; adult birds frequently defend their territory and nest at any time of the day or year.]]
In the heart of their distribution, i.e. east Africa, breeding activity in this species can peak any time from February to September, but can occur nearly any month at the species level.<ref name= Konig/><ref>[https://web.archive.org/web/20011012201238/http://www.owls.org/Species/bubo/verreauxs_eagle_owl.htm Verreaux's Eagle-Owl]. Owls.org (2012-08-19). Retrieved on 2012-08-24.</ref> The timing of breeding is said to be correspondent roughly to the regional dry season, so averages earlier in the northern part of the range (before February) and later (July to September) in the southern part of the range such as [[Kenya]] and [[South Africa]].<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/> In the northern part of the range, breeding season commenced in November in [[Mali]], in November and December in [[Senegal]], December in [[Equatorial Guinea]] and January in [[Nigeria]].<ref name= Wilson/><ref>Mackworth-Praed, C. W., & Grant, C. H. B. (1970). ''Birds of West Central Africa Vol I''.</ref> The monogamous pair is quite stable, most likely mating for life. As in most owls, a [[courtship display]] is both to establish mates for a newly mature pair of eagle-owls or to strength pair bonds prior to nesting.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Hume/><ref name= Steyn2/> Vocalizations during courtship displays consist of relatively rapid and excited calling, hooting and whining. The pair during courtship will bow to one another, flick open their wings and preen each other's feathers, with the male taking the more active part in the courtship ritual.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Hume/> Like all raptorial birds, Verreaux's eagle-owls are strongly territorial.<ref name= Steyn/> The pair will defend their territory by their song and sometimes (though rarely) through duets. The territories of Verreaux's eagle-owls can range up to 7,000 hectares in size, although average territory sizes are seemingly unknown.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Hume/><ref name= Steyn/>

Like [[great horned owl]]s, but unlike [[Eurasian eagle-owl]]s, the Verreaux's eagle-owls normally uses old nests built by other birds as their own nests. Usage of a nest site other than those constructed by other birds is considered rarer even than in the horned owl and is viewed as almost exceptional in some parts of this species range.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Brown/><ref name= Wilson/> Existent reports of this species building its own nest are certain to be dubious, as no known living owl builds a nest and only a small handful of owl species have been verified adding a small amount of nesting material to an existing surface or nest.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Brown/> The variety of bird nests they use is extreme. Large stick nests in sturdy trees are generally used.<ref name= Konig/> In southern Africa, recorded nest heights have ranged from {{convert|6|to|25|m|ftin|0|abbr=on}} off the ground.<ref name= Steyn/> Like other ''Bubo'' owls, the large nest of large-bodied [[Accipitridae|accipitrids]] are often popular for use, due to the often huge size and sturdiness of construction typical in this family, with the nest builders devoting up to four months to their construction.<ref>Collias, N. E., & Collias, E. C. (2014). ''Nest building and bird behavior''. Princeton University Press.</ref> However, perhaps the constructor of nests that most often host Verreaux's eagle-owls are [[hamerkop]]s (''Scopus umbretta''). In everywhere from [[Mali]] to [[South Africa]] the eagle-owl has been recorded using old nests built by this species.<ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/> The unusual, massive nest is an enclosed circle of sticks with a side entrance that are often very large relative to the size of the hamerkop, a smallish, compact wading bird. Usually the eagle-owls nest on the flat top of the hamerkop nest rather than the interior which is usually too small for the eagle-owls to enter and this can provide a rather safe structure for the eagle-owl family to call home.<ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/>

Other nest builders which are popular as hosts are [[Old World vulture|vultures]], [[eagle]]s (at least eight species have built nests used by these eagle-owls), [[secretarybird]]s, [[crow]]s and even much smaller birds such as [[Ploceidae|weaver]]s, which build huge communal nest structures which the eagle-owls then similarly nest on top of.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Brown/><ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/><ref>Oschadleus, H. D. (2019). ''Raptors breeding on weaver nests in trees and on man-made structures''. ''Ostrich'', 90(1), 85-88.</ref> Most nests are already abandoned when the Verreaux's eagle-owl take over it, in large accipitrids for example, many build alternate nests which are not used for years on end.<ref name= Steyn/> However, if the nest is occupied, the Verreaux's eagle-owl pair readily displaces the occupants and sometimes feeds on the birds in them.<ref name= Steyn/> Species known to be successfully displaced from their nests have ranged up in size to [[lappet-faced vulture]]s (''Torgos tracheliotos''), which are more than three times heavier on average than the Verreaux's eagle-owl.<ref name= Steyn/> In some cases, hamerkops have been known to try to defend their nest from the eagle-owls but are usually chased away.<ref name= Brown/> Verreaux's eagle-owls have been known to displace other opportunistic nest usurpers such as other [[owl]]s and [[falcon]]s in order to take over nest structures for themselves.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Wilson/><ref>Tarboton, W. R., & Erasmus, R. (1998). ''Sasol Owls & Owling in Southern Africa''. Struik.</ref> In one case, a pair of eagle-owls nested on top of a hamerkop nest while the interior of the nest was occupied by [[Egyptian geese]] (''Alopochen aegyptiacus''), an unusual aggressive species of waterfowl that uses nests built by other species.<ref name= Steyn/> In rare cases, Verreaux's eagle-owls have been recorded using large, old hollows, the stem of a [[Arecaceae|palm]] tree or on a very dense tangle of creepers or orchids instead of birds' nests as a nesting site.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Diversity/>

On average, the female lays two white eggs, which typically measure {{convert|62.6|x|51.4|mm|in|abbr=on}}, with a range in height of {{convert|58|to|66|mm|in|abbr=on}} and a range in width of {{convert|48|to|54|mm|in|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/><ref>Archer, G. and Goodman, EM (1961) ''The birds of British Somaliland and the Gulf of Aden''. Vol. III. Oliver & Boyd, Edinburgh.</ref> The eggs weigh from {{convert|93|to|101.6|g|oz|abbr=on}}, the upper weight being the mean mass of the first egg and the lower weight being the mean mass of the second egg.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Mikkola/> The eggs are reportedly laid at up to 7 day intervals and may take up nearly seven days as well between hatching.<ref name= Konig/> Most nest reportedly contain two eggs, but some may contain only one, and no more than two has been recorded in this species.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/><ref>Moreau, R. E. (1944). ''Clutch‐size: a comparative study, with special reference to African birds''. ''Ibis'', 86(3), 286-347.</ref> The adult female incubates the eggs for 33 to 39 days, the incubation stage being slightly longer than those of most other eagle-owls, at least the more northern species.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Wilson/> On average at hatching, the young weigh about {{convert|60|to|70|g|oz|abbr=on}}.<ref name= Konig/> The weight of the nestling can triple within five days after hatching.<ref name= Wilson/> Due to the extreme interval between the hatching of the first and the second egg, the older owlet is always considerably larger than the second. As is widely reported in different kinds of raptorial birds, the smaller chick usually dies in the nest.<ref>Simmons, R. (1988). ''Offspring quality and the evolution of cainism''. ''Ibis'', 130(3), 339-357.</ref> This may be due to starvation upon being outcompeted for food by the older chick or the smaller chick may be being attacked and [[Siblicide|killed]] by its older sibling. Usually the smaller chick is gone within two weeks after hatching in this species.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Fry/> In rare cases, both chicks are reared and survive to leave the nest, although there are no known cases of two fledglings resulting from a Verreaux's eagle-owl nest in southern Africa.<ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/> The young are covered in off-white down from hatching on and the pink eyelids may become apparent within the first week of life.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Wilson/> By three weeks of age, the chicks down will thicken and darken to a greyish colour with some barring present.<ref name= Steyn/> By six weeks, the young eagle-owl will start to somewhat resemble an adult, replete with the blackish brackets on the facial disc of the adult but still being fairly downy, particularly about the head. Only a week later, almost all the down is likely to be moulted.<ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn/>

The mother Verreaux's eagle-owl remains on the nest for nearly the entire incubation period while the male hunts for food for both of them. During the brooding stage, which lasts about 20 days after hatching, the female is still fed by the male, but resumes hunting thereafter.<ref name= Konig/> During the incubation and brooding stage, the male usually roosts near the nest during the day while the female continually sits about the nest.<ref name= Steyn/> After the brooding stage, the female normally takes to a perch within a dozen or so metres of the nest.<ref name= Wilson/> Both parents may use a favor perch near the nest at which they dismantle prey into pieces that can be more easily consumed by their young, these may be called "plucking" perches where birds are more commonly eaten or "peeling" perches where hedgehogs are the most regular prey. Most dietary studies for the species have been from researching the pellets and skins under such perches.<ref name= Steyn/><ref name= Fry/> The female is an extremely tight sitter both while incubating and brooding, and may not even be displaced from the nest even if shouted at or the tree is struck.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn/> When intruders approach too closely, including other eagle-owls, potential predators and humans, the most common response of the parent Verreaux's eagle-owl is to grunt lowly, often raising its ear-tufts and bill-clapping.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Brown/> Both sexes may engage in distraction displays when the area near the nest is encroached, but it usually the male and most displays occur during nighttime but are possible at any time of day or night. During such displays, the adult will fly over the ground with drooping wings, or alights and drags its wings and flaps about, often while bill-clacking and calling.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn/> Similar injury-feigning distraction displays have been recorded in the [[Eurasian eagle-owl]] and smaller owl species but are not known in most other ''Bubo'' species.<ref name= Voous/><ref>Witherby, H. F., Jourdian, F.C.R., Ticehurst, N. F., and Tucker, B. W (1943). ''The Handbook of British Birds''. ''Nature'', 140, 199-200.</ref> In one case, feral dogs were successfully lured away from a young Verreaux's eagle-owl by its parents' distraction display after the young bird had fallen to the ground.<ref name= Steyn/> In rare cases, the parent eagle-owls will attack interlopers. In one such case, a person who picked up a young eagle-owl on the ground was severely injured after both parents attacked him.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/>

[[File:Verreaux's Eagle-owl (Bubo lacteus) chick watching a moth (7011236027).jpg|thumb|A Verreaux's eagle-owl "fledgling" such as this one often leaves the nest well before it can fly competently.]]
On average, the young Verreaux's eagle-owl leaves the nest at around 62–63 days but cannot fly at this point. It may take roughly anywhere from another two weeks to a month after this before the fledgling is a competent flier.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/> After leaving the nest, the fledgling is "remarkably inactive", making a minimum of effort to fly, and usually selecting a roost within a few feet of the nest which it has awkwardly climbed to or will drop to a large bush below the nest.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/> In the nest, the chick will beg for food with a shrill or chittering noise, sometimes bobbing its head or swaying about and transferring its weight between its feet (sometimes called a "hunger dance") and it continues to rely on its parents for food well after leaving the nest.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn/> Sometimes after leaving the nest, the young eagle-owls are mobbed as are adults by other birds of prey and crows during the day, which is often heatedly directed at this species as adult eagle-owls regularly kill these birds at night. The young eagle-owl may dodge to denser branches to avoid being wounded during such attacks.<ref name= Brown/> Young Verreaux's eagle-owls may fall to the ground, often as a result of mobbing. If the young bird is discovered on the ground, it may feign death, lying prone with its head lax and its eyes closed. Even if picked up while death-shamming, the young eagle-owl may remain moribund. Upon being left without disturbance after "playing dead", the young Verreaux's eagle-owl will gradually open its eyes and return to a normal state.<ref name= Steyn/>

It is not until they are about 5 months old do most young Verreaux's eagle-owl show the ability to capture prey for themselves. However, the stage at which the young of this species becomes independent appears remarkably variable. One ringed 9-month-old moved {{convert|24|km|mi|abbr=on}} away from its nest area and was thus seemingly fully independent.<ref name= Konig/> On the other hand, Verreaux's eagle-owls of over half-a-year in age who presumably can fly and hunt on their own have been seen to linger and continue to beg its parents to be fed into the next breeding season, and may even be fed by their father while he is also feeding the mother and a new nestling.<ref name= Steyn/> In [[Kenya]], when a biologist fed a wild juvenile eagle-owl [[mole-rat]]s and chicken heads in its nest area, the young eagle-owl apparently became remarkably confiding towards the person.<ref name= Pitman/> The tendency of young eagle-owls to linger into the next breeding season sometimes results in "family groups" roosting together, a very unusual occurrence for an eagle-owl species. One such group consisted of five birds together, including two parents and three owls from the preceding past three years and apparently the younger eagle-owls even helped bring food for the chick once the egg hatched.<ref name= Hume/><ref name= Pitman/><ref name= Steyn/>

On average, [[sexual maturity]] in Verreaux's eagle-owls appears to be attained at three to four years of age.<ref name= Konig/> In most cases, a pair of Verreaux's eagle-owl is able to nest annually; however, in some cases they may nest only every two to three years, in probable situations of extreme food shortages.<ref name= Konig/><ref name= Steyn/> Annual mortality appears to be fairly low in this large owl species. Few species have been reported to hunt Verreaux's eagle-owls short of the aforementioned jackal attack, even nests have rarely been seen to be predated, although they may on rare occasions run foul of some predators such as larger [[felid]]s with the ability to climb.<ref name= Pitman/><ref name= Steyn/> That young birds usually leave the nest before they can fly would appear to endanger them but the threat and distraction display of parent eagle-owls are apparently often successful.<ref name= Steyn/> Adult eagle-owls can appear nearly fearless, as they have been reported to stand their ground and engage in threat displays when encountered on or near the ground against much larger animals such as [[rhinoceros]]es and [[lion]]s, and in such cases are apparently not approached further by the bigger animals although the eagle-owls could easily be killed by such animals if contact was made.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Pitman/> The lifespan in the wild is not known; however, in captivity the species can live for over 15 years, and possibly up to 30 years in some cases.<ref name= Konig/> In a Hungarian zoo (in [[Balatonederics]]) lives a specimen originally acquired in [[Tanzania]] in 1968, as of 2025, it is 57 years old.<ref>{{Cite web |title=Preparátumnak nézték, majd kinyitotta szemét a vén afrikai bagoly a múzeumban |url=https://likebalaton.hu/telepules/balatonederics/hireink/preparatumnak-neztek-majd-kinyitotta-szemet-a-ven-afrikai-bagoly-a-muzeumban-329621/ |access-date=2025-11-21 |website=LikeBalaton |language=hu}}</ref>

==Status==

[[File:Bubo lacteus 1838.jpg|thumb|left|An artist's rendering of a Verreaux's eagle-owl from 1838]]
Verreaux's eagle-owl is a seldom-encountered species, occurring at low densities and needing large territories for hunting and breeding purposes. The threats faced by this species are sadly typical of many large birds of prey from around the world. Not infrequently, they are locally rare due to persecution.<ref name= Hume/><ref name="Penhallurick"/> The normal cause of persecution is their possible status as predators of small domestic stock, though this is certain to be rare, at least in areas with substantial wild prey populations.<ref name= Hume/><ref>Enriquez, P. A., & Mikkola, H. (1997). ''Comparative study of general public owl knowledge in Costa Rica, Central America and Malawi, Africa''.</ref> An additional threat is the residual effects of pesticides, as poison (usually through rodenticide or poisoned carcasses left out for scavengers such as jackals) consumed through prey may badly affect them.<ref name= Konig/><ref>Herholdt, J. J. (1998). ''Survival threats and conservation management of raptors in the Kgalagadi Transfrontier Park''. ''Transactions of the Royal Society of South Africa'', 53(2), 201-218.</ref> They may be killed by flying into novel man-made objects, including wires and massive dams along reservoirs.<ref>Cooper, J. E. (1973). ''Post-mortem findings in East African birds of prey''. ''Journal of Wildlife Diseases'', 9(4), 368-375.</ref><ref>van Rooyen, C. & Diamond, M. (2008). ''Wildlife-Powerline Interaction Management''. ''Indwa'', 6: 7-16.</ref><ref>Anderson, M. D., Maritz, A. W., & Oosthuysen, E. (1999). ''Raptors drowning in farm reservoirs in South Africa''. ''Ostrich'', 70(2), 139-144.</ref>

[[Habitat destruction]] can also affect them, as they require ample trees with large bird nests in order to take residence in a given area.<ref name= Hume/><ref>Herremans, M. (1998). ''Conservation status of birds in Botswana in relation to land use''. ''Biological Conservation'', 86(2), 139-160.</ref> In some areas, however, they've been shown to be able to nest in [[Peri-urbanisation|peri-urban]] or suburban areas, showing greater adaptability to human-based land changes than many other large birds of prey.<ref name= Chittenden/><ref name= Fry/> In [[Swaziland|Eswatini]], the species is considered Near Threatened and the species has been recommended for threatened status in southern Africa overall.<ref>Monadjem, A., Boycott, R.C., Parker, V., & Culverwell, J. 2003. ''Threatened vertebrates of Swaziland. Swaziland Red Data Book: fishes, amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals''. Ministry of Tourism, Environment and Communications, Mbabane, Swaziland.</ref><ref>Monadjem, A., & Rasmussen, M. W. (2008). ''Nest distribution and conservation status of eagles, selected hawks and owls in Swaziland''. ''Gabar'', 18, 1-22.</ref><ref>Jenkins, A. R. (2008). ''A proposed new list of the threatened raptors of southern Africa''. ''Gabar'', 19(1), 27-40.</ref> In west Africa and central Africa, the habitat is often marginal for this species, the distribution is sporadic and thus this eagle-owl is only encountered either uncommonly or rarely.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name= Wilson/><ref name= Steyn2/> The greatest regional stronghold for Verreaux's eagle-owls is seemingly east Africa, in countries such as [[Kenya]], which may have numbers comparable to pre-colonial times.<ref name= Brown/><ref name= Steyn2/> At the species level, they are widespread and currently not considered to be threatened with extinction.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /><ref name= Konig/>
